message.content:
You are verifier #1, independent. Read the ACTUAL code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay and decide if this is a TRUE production-readiness problem in current code (6 passes hardened). confirmed=true only if real, reproducible, worth fixing (not speculative/by-design).
J-002: Operator full display name ('Thomas Jones') is not scrubbed — leaks in 252 committed transcript files alongside redacted emails
File: scripts/capture_transcripts.py:38
VERIFIED. _SECRET_PATTERNS + _USERNAME_RE redact the operator's email and the bare home-dir username ('[REDACTED]'), and mask the username inside paths/slugs, but do NOT redact the operator's real full DISPLAY NAME ('Thomas Jones'), which is distinct operator PII appearing via git author strings, pyproject authors=[], and prose. Confirmed live and already committed: `grep -rc 'Thomas Jones' transcripts/` returns 252 files with the name in cleartext, frequently directly beside a successfully-redacted email (e.g. `Author: Thomas Jones <[REDACTED]>`). `git log -1 --format=%an` returns 'Thomas Jones', confirming this is the operator identity, not a coincidental token. De-identifying the email while leaving the adjacent full name defeats the de-identification purpose of the #F-024/#G-007 work. No test in tests/test_scripts.py covers full-name redaction (0 matches), confirming an unintentional gap rather than a documented scope decision.
PoC: 1) cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay  2) git log -1 --format='%an' -> 'Thomas Jones' (operator PII)  3) grep -rc 'Thomas Jones' transcripts/ -> 252 files with count >=1 (cleartext)  4) _scrub('Author: Thomas Jones <a@b.c>') -> 'Author: Thomas Jones <[REDACTED]>' (email masked, full name leaks).
Fix: Derive the operator display name (from `git config user.name` and/or a configurable ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES env list) and add it to the redaction set, mirroring _username_pattern(): re.escape'd, word-boundaried, length-guarded, applied in _scrub() (skip if empty/too-short to avoid over-redacting common words). Add a test_scripts.py case asserting _scrub('Author: Thomas Jones <a@b.c>') masks BOTH name and email. Because the leak is already committed, re-run capture (or scrub history) to remediate existing transcripts/ files.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this finding by examining the actual code.

content.content:
1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	"""Capture the active Claude session's JSONL transcript(s) into the repo for provenance.
3	
4	Run by the pre-commit hook (.githooks/pre-commit) so every commit pulls in an up-to-date
5	snapshot of the agent interactions/effort behind it — an auditable record alongside the work.
6	
7	What it captures: the *active* session (the most-recently-modified top-level transcript in the
8	Claude project dir) plus that session's ``<id>/`` subtree (subagents + workflow transcripts).
9	Other/unrelated sessions are left out. Credential-shaped secrets are redacted on the way in.
10	
11	Honest scope: transcripts still contain local filesystem paths and may contain the operator's
12	email and internal reasoning. If this repo is published, review/exclude ``transcripts/`` first
13	(see transcripts/README.md). The wheel never ships them (only ``src/assay_engine`` is packaged).
14	
15	Config: ASSAY_TRANSCRIPT_SRC overrides the source dir. Exits 0 on any error (never blocks a
16	commit).
17	"""
18	
19	from __future__ import annotations
20	
21	import datetime as _dt
22	import hashlib
23	import json
24	import os
25	import re
26	import sys
27	from pathlib import Path
28	
29	DEFAULT_SRC = Path.home() / ".claude" / "projects" / "-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc"
30	DEST = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "transcripts" / "sessions"
31	MANIFEST = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "transcripts" / "MANIFEST.json"
32	
33	# Credential-shaped secrets to redact (defense). Pass 3 (#F-024) extends this to operator PII —
34	# email addresses and the home-directory username embedded in filesystem paths — so committing
35	# transcripts as provenance (the campaign mandate) does not also publish the operator's identity.
36	# Reasoning content and the path STRUCTURE remain (legitimate provenance); see the README caveat
37	# for the full public-release consideration.
38	_SECRET_PATTERNS = [
39	    re.compile(r"sk-ant-[A-Za-z0-9_-]{2,}"),  # any sk-ant- token incl. oat01 OAuth + placeholders
40	    re.compile(r"\bgh[posru]_[A-Za-z0-9]{20,}\b"),  # GitHub tokens
41	    re.compile(r"\bAKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}\b"),  # AWS access key id
42	    # bearer tokens incl. base64url/base64 JWTs — must cover + / = or the token is split at the
43	    # first such char and only the leading fragment is redacted (pass 3, #F-051).
44	    re.compile(r"(?i)bearer\s+[A-Za-z0-9._\-+/=]{20,}"),
45	    # explicit OAuth/token env values: "...OAUTH_TOKEN":"<value>" / ...KEY=<value>
46	    re.compile(r'((?:OAUTH_TOKEN|_TOKEN|_KEY|_SECRET)"\s*:\s*")[^"]{8,}(")'),
47	    re.compile(r"((?:OAUTH_TOKEN|_TOKEN|_KEY|_SECRET)=)[^\s\"']{8,}"),
48	    # operator PII (#F-024): email addresses, and the username segment of a home path
49	    # (/Users/[REDACTED] /home/[REDACTED] — the path STRUCTURE is kept, only the identity is masked.
50	    re.compile(r"\b[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+\.[A-Za-z]{2,}\b"),  # email → [REDACTED]
51	    re.compile(r"(/(?:Users|home)/)[^/\s\"']+"),  # /Users/[REDACTED] → /Users/[REDACTED]    # The Claude project-dir SLUG (e.g. -Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc): redact the username segment
53	    # regardless of length (#H-018) — the bare-token rule below skips short handles to avoid
54	    # over-redacting common words, but the slug form is unambiguous PII at any length.
55	    re.compile(r"(-(?:Users|home)-)[^-\s/\"']+"),  # -Users-[REDACTED]- → -Users-[REDACTED]-
56	]
57	
58	
59	def _username_pattern() -> re.Pattern[str] | None:
60	    """A redaction for the bare operator username wherever it appears (#G-007).
61	
62	    The /Users|/home path rule only masks the username inside a path; the same handle leaks via
63	    git author strings, prose, and the Claude project-dir slug (``-Users-[REDACTED]-...``). Derive the
64	    handle from the home directory and redact it as a standalone token too. Skipped for trivially
65	    short/None handles (e.g. a CI 'root') to avoid over-redacting common words.
66	    """
67	    try:
68	        name = Path.home().name
69	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - never break capture over a missing home
70	        return None
71	    if not name or len(name) < 4:
72	        return None
73	    return re.compile(re.escape(name))
74	
75	
76	_USERNAME_RE = _username_pattern()
77	
78	
79	def _scrub(text: str) -> str:
80	    for pat in _SECRET_PATTERNS:
81	        if pat.groups == 2:
82	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]\2", text)
83	        elif pat.groups == 1:
84	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]", text)
85	        else:
86	            text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
87	    # Redact the bare operator username everywhere (#G-007), after the path rule so masked paths
88	    # don't re-expose it. Done last so it also catches it inside project-dir slugs / git authors.
89	    if _USERNAME_RE is not None:
90	        text = _USERNAME_RE.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
91	    return text
92	
93	
94	def _active_session(src: Path) -> Path | None:
95	    """The most-recently-modified top-level transcript = the live session."""
96	    candidates = sorted(src.glob("*.jsonl"), key=lambda p: p.stat().st_mtime, reverse=True)
97	    return candidates[0] if candidates else None
98	
99	
100	def _copy_scrubbed(srcf: Path, dstf: Path) -> dict | None:
101	    try:
102	        dstf.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
103	        raw = srcf.read_bytes()
104	        # Scrub EVERY text file, not only .jsonl (#CV-O-1): the session subtree also holds .json
105	        # workflow/subagent transcripts that carry the same operator PII + credential-shaped
106	        # secrets — the old suffix==".jsonl" gate copied those RAW, bypassing redaction entirely.
107	        # Decode strictly to classify: text → scrub; genuinely binary (rare here) → copy verbatim.
108	        try:
109	            text = raw.decode("utf-8")
110	        except UnicodeDecodeError:
111	            data = raw  # binary blob — nothing to scrub
112	        else:
113	            data = _scrub(text).encode("utf-8")
114	        if dstf.exists() and dstf.read_bytes() == data:
115	            pass  # unchanged — still report it in the manifest
116	        else:
117	            dstf.write_bytes(data)
118	        return {
119	            "path": str(dstf.relative_to(DEST.parent.parent)),
120	            "bytes": len(data),
121	            "sha256": hashlib.sha256(data).hexdigest(),
122	        }
123	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 — provenance capture must never break a commit
124	        return None
125	
126	
127	def main() -> int:
128	    src = Path(os.environ.get("ASSAY_TRANSCRIPT_SRC", str(DEFAULT_SRC)))
129	    if not src.is_dir():
130	        return 0
131	    session = _active_session(src)
132	    if session is None:
133	        return 0
134	    sid = session.stem
135	    captured: list[dict] = []
136	
137	    rec = _copy_scrubbed(session, DEST / f"{sid}.jsonl")
138	    if rec:
139	        captured.append(rec)
140	
141	    # the session's subtree: subagents/ + workflows/ transcripts
142	    subtree = src / sid
143	    if subtree.is_dir():
144	        for f in sorted(subtree.rglob("*")):
145	            if f.is_file():
146	                rel = f.relative_to(subtree)
147	                rec = _copy_scrubbed(f, DEST / sid / rel)
148	                if rec:
149	                    captured.append(rec)
150	
151	    try:
152	        MANIFEST.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
153	        MANIFEST.write_text(
154	            json.dumps(
155	                {
156	                    "captured_at": _dt.datetime.now(tz=_dt.timezone.utc).isoformat(),
157	                    "active_session": sid,
158	                    "n_files": len(captured),
159	                    "total_bytes": sum(c["bytes"] for c in captured),
160	                    "files": captured,
161	                },
162	                indent=2,
163	            )
164	            + "\n",
165	            encoding="utf-8",
166	        )
167	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001
168	        return 0
169	    print(f"captured {len(captured)} transcript file(s) for session {sid}")
170	    return 0
171	
172	
173	if __name__ == "__main__":
174	    sys.exit(main())
175

content.text: The finding is confirmed at the raw level. Let me verify the redaction behavior and that the name appears alongside redacted emails, and check for any test coverage.

import sys; sys.path.insert(0,'scripts')

print(repr(_scrub('Author: Thomas Jones <a@b.c>')))
"
echo "---adjacent redacted---"
grep -rh 'Thomas Jones' transcripts/ 2>/dev/null | grep -i 'REDACTED' | head -3

content.content:
'Author: Thomas Jones <a@b.c>'
---adjacent redacted---
{"parentUuid":"fb08f2d5-9a89-4b5c-ab7e-94d148d65052","isSidechain":true,"promptId":"5026518e-7dcc-4a0b-9a5e-80d20477306e","agentId":"a4a13c7923535378a","type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01CMtHitSMcp8PBpsbYvNAvR","type":"tool_result","content":"title:	Firewall A (ClaimBlindGuard) is advisory, not structural — claim-blindness not enforced in code
state:	OPEN
author:	thehipsterciso (Thomas Jones)
labels:	audit-pass-1, needs-adjudication, sev-high
comments:	0
assignees:	
projects:	
milestone:	
issue-type:	
parent:	
sub-issues:	
sub-issues-completed:	
blocked-by:	
blocking:	
number:	1
--
**Source:** Independent adversarial audit, pass 1. Convergent: flagged by 2 of 4 independent auditors.
**Severity:** high
**Location:** `src/assay_engine/methodology/firewalls.py:28-66`; `src/assay_engine/baseline/toolkit.py:35-41`

**Claim:** Firewall A (claim-blindness) is advisory, not structural. `ClaimBlindGuard.sealed()` only flips a private boolean; it has effect *only if* a baseline builder voluntarily routes a read through `guard.access(...)`. A builder that reads the claims object by any other path is unguarded while sealed. ADR-0005 / ARCHITECTURE.md §3 promise claim-blindness is "enforced in code... not just promised" — this implementation is precisely the "good intentions" the ADR says to replace with structure.

**Evidence:** Repro — inside `with guard.sealed():`, a builder that ignores `claim_guard` and reads claims smuggled via `corpus.metadata` returns them with no `FirewallViolation`. The docstring itself concedes "the baseline builder calls guard.access(...) defensively." The only genuine structural barrier is the `build(corpus, *, claim_guard)` signature (no claims source passed) — the guard adds appearance, not enforcement.

**Suggested fix:** Make the guard custodial — have it hold the claims source and hand baseline code only a sealed proxy/None whose sole un-sealing path is `access()`; or wrap the claims source in a sealable proxy whose methods raise while sealed. Add a test that a non-cooperating builder cannot reach claims. Alternatively, downgrade the docstring/ADR language to "defensive assertion + structural separation at the StudyDefinition layer."

---
_Pass-1 candidate finding. Awaiting independent true/false-positive adjudication (posted as a comment by an agent that did not author this finding)._

---grep firewall/leak enforcement---

]

content.text:
The finding is fully confirmed by reproduction:

1. `_scrub('Author: Thomas Jones <a@b.c>')` returns `'Author: Thomas Jones <a@b.c>'` — interestingly, even the email is NOT redacted here because the `b.c` TLD is only 1 char, but importantly "Thomas Jones" is preserved verbatim regardless.
2. The committed transcripts show clear cases: `email="[REDACTED]"` and `cwd="/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-grc"` are masked, but `{ name = "Thomas Jones", email = "[REDACTED]" }` and `author: thehipsterciso (Thomas Jones)` keep the full name in cleartext directly beside redacted fields.
3. 252 files contain "Thomas Jones" in cleartext.
4. Zero test coverage for name redaction.

This is a real, reproducible de-identification gap. The whole point of the #F-024/#G-007 work was to remove operator identity, yet the full display name — distinct PII confirmed as the operator via `git log` — leaks alongside successfully redacted emails/usernames. Already committed.

On severity: this is operator PII leakage in an already-committed, potentially-publishable repo. The README does carry an honest caveat about reviewing transcripts before publishing, and the wheel never ships transcripts, which caps the severity. But the explicit, code-implemented de-identification pipeline silently leaves a hole that defeats its own stated purpose. Medium.

input.reasoning: Reproduced against current code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay. scripts/capture_transcripts.py:38-91 redacts the operator email, the bare home-dir username ('[REDACTED]'), and the username segment in paths/slugs, but never the operator's real full display name. git log -1 --format=%an returns 'Thomas Jones', confirming this is the operator identity. grep -rc 'Thomas Jones' transcripts/ returns 252 committed files containing it in cleartext. The name appears directly beside successfully-redacted fields in committed transcripts (e.g. pyproject `{ name = "Thomas Jones", email = "[REDACTED]" }`, GitHub issue `author: thehipsterciso (Thomas Jones)`, and cwd=/Users/[REDACTED]/...), so the masking of email/username is undercut by the adjacent cleartext name. No test in tests/ covers display-name redaction (0 matches for 'Thomas Jones'/user.name/SCRUB_NAMES), confirming an unintentional gap rather than a documented scope decision. The leak is real, reproducible, and contradicts the stated purpose of the #F-024/#G-007 de-identification work. The suggested fix is sound: derive the display name from `git config user.name` and/or a configurable env list, escape + word-boundary + length-guard it (mirroring _username_pattern), apply in _scrub, add a regression test, and re-capture/scrub history to remediate the 252 already-committed files. Severity is bounded below critical/high: the README documents the transcripts-contain-PII caveat and instructs review before publishing, the wheel never ships transcripts, and exposure requires publishing the repo. But it is a genuine, code-level de-identification hole leaking distinct operator PII into committed history, warranting a fix — medium.

content.text: Confirmed J-002 as a true medium-severity production-readiness issue. The operator's full display name "Thomas Jones" leaks in 252 committed transcript files at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/transcripts/, despite the de-identification pipeline in /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/capture_transcripts.py (lines 38-91) successfully redacting the adjacent email and username. Reproduced live; no test coverage exists for name redaction.
